summary: Gruber pushes back against a Washington Post story arguing that Apple copies features from top App Store apps. He contends that adding obvious features like period tracking to the Health app or a flashlight to the OS is natural platform evolution, not theft. He argues that successful third-party developers have always thrived by going deeper than Apple's broad-appeal defaults, pointing to Mail, Podcasts, and Calendar as categories where third parties coexist with Apple's built-in apps. He notes that the 'Sherlocking' debate predates app stores entirely and has nothing to do with them.
key_points: Platform vendors adding basic features to the OS is inevitable and expected — developers should factor this into their business models
Apple's built-in apps aim for broadest appeal, leaving room for third-party apps that serve power users or niche needs
The Sherlock/Watson controversy happened in 2002, years before app stores existed, proving this debate isn't an App Store problem
The more basic and obvious a feature is, the more likely the platform vendor will eventually build it in
Successful third-party apps like Things, OmniFocus, and many podcast apps thrive alongside Apple's defaults
money_quotes: What's the alternative? For Apple never to add any features to the OS that exist in third-party apps?
Of course fertility and period-tracking should be features in Apple's Health app. If anything they should have been there sooner.

One of the factors every successful developer considers is whether or not an idea might be something Apple (or Google, or Microsoft, or whoever the platform vendor is) might incorporate as a feature in the system.
The key to competing with Apple as a third-party developer is to focus on segments of the audience that want more than the basics.
The debate over what's fair game for Apple (or Google, or Microsoft) to copy from third-party developers has nothing to do with app stores.
When a utility is designed to compensate for a hole in Mac OS X, the developer should not expect the hole to remain unfilled by Apple forever.
key_insight: Platform vendors filling obvious feature gaps in their own OS is natural evolution, not predatory copying, and smart developers compete by going deeper than the defaults.
